chapter i in chancery london
michaelmas term lately over and the lord chancellor sit in lincoln s inn hall
implacable november weather
as much mud in the street as if the water have but newly retire from the face of the earth and it would not be wonderful to meet a megalosaurus forty foot long or so waddle like an elephantine lizard up holborn hill
smoke lower down from chimney-pot make a soft black drizzle with flake of soot in it as big as full-grow snowflake--go into mourning one might imagine for the death of the sun
dog undistinguishable in mire
horse scarcely well splash to their very blinker
foot passenger jostle one another s umbrella in a general infection of ill temper and lose their foot-hold at street-corner where ten of thousand of other foot passenger have be slip and slide since the day break if this day ever break add new deposit to the crust upon crust of mud stick at those point tenaciously to the pavement and accumulate at compound interest
fog everywhere
fog up the river where it flow among green ait and meadow fog down the river where it roll defile among the tier of shipping and the waterside pollution of a great and dirty city
fog on the essex marsh fog on the kentish height
fog creep into the caboose of collier-brig fog lie out on the yard and hover in the rigging of great ship fog drooping on the gunwale of barge and small boat
fog in the eye and throat of ancient greenwich pensioner wheeze by the fireside of their ward fog in the stem and bowl of the afternoon pipe of the wrathful skipper down in his close cabin fog cruelly pinch the toe and finger of his shiver little prentice boy on deck
chance people on the bridge peep over the parapet into a nether sky of fog with fog all round they as if they be up in a balloon and hang in the misty cloud
gas loom through the fog in diver place in the street much as the sun may from the spongey field be see to loom by husbandman and ploughboy
most of the shop light two hour before their time--as the gas seem to know for it have a haggard and unwilling look
the raw afternoon be raw and the dense fog be dense and the muddy street be muddy near that leaden-head old obstruction appropriate ornament for the threshold of a leaden-head old corporation temple bar
and hard by temple bar in lincoln s inn hall at the very heart of the fog sit the lord high chancellor in his high court of chancery
never can there come fog too thick never can there come mud and mire too deep to assort with the groping and flounder condition which this high court of chancery most pestilent of hoary sinner hold this day in the sight of heaven and earth
on such an afternoon if ever the lord high chancellor ought to be sit here--as here he be--with a foggy glory round his head softly fence in with crimson cloth and curtain address by a large advocate with great whisker a little voice and an interminable brief and outwardly direct his contemplation to the lantern in the roof where he can see nothing but fog
on such an afternoon some score of member of the high court of chancery bar ought to be--as here they be--mistily engage in one of the ten thousand stage of an endless cause trip one another up on slippery precedent grope knee-deep in technicality run their goat-hair and horsehair ward head against wall of word and make a pretence of equity with serious face as player might
on such an afternoon the various solicitor in the cause some two or three of whom have inherit it from their father who make a fortune by it ought to be--as be they not range in a line in a long matted well but you might look in vain for truth at the bottom of it between the registrar s red table and the silk gown with bill cross-bill answer rejoinder injunction affidavit issue reference to master master report mountain of costly nonsense pile before they
well may the court be dim with waste candle here and there well may the fog hang heavy in it as if it would never get out well may the stain-glass window lose their colour and admit no light of day into the place well may the uninitiate from the street who peep in through the glass pane in the door be deter from entrance by its owlish aspect and by the drawl languidly echo to the roof from the pad dais where the lord high chancellor look into the lantern that have no light in it and where the attendant wig be all stick in a fog-bank
this be the court of chancery which have its decay house and its blighted land in every shire which have its wear-out lunatic in every madhouse and its dead in every churchyard which have its ruin suitor with his slipshod heel and threadbare dress borrowing and beg through the round of every man s acquaintance which give to monied might the mean abundantly of weary out the right which so exhaust finance patience courage hope so overthrow the brain and break the heart that there be not an honourable man among its practitioner who would not give--who do not often give--the warning suffer any wrong that can be do you rather than come here who happen to be in the lord chancellor s court this murky afternoon besides the lord chancellor the counsel in the cause two or three counsel who be never in any cause and the well of solicitor before mention

these be all yawn for no crumb of amusement ever fall from jarndyce and jarndyce the cause in hand which be squeeze dry year upon year ago
the short-hand writer the reporter of the court and the reporter of the newspaper invariably decamp with the rest of the regular when jarndyce and jarndyce come on
their place be a blank
stand on a seat at the side of the hall the well to peer into the curtain sanctuary be a little mad old woman in a squeeze bonnet who be always in court from its sit to its rise and always expect some incomprehensible judgment to be give in her favour
some say she really be or be a party to a suit but no one know for certain because no one care
she carry some small litter in a reticule which she call her document principally consist of paper match and dry lavender

in the meantime his prospect in life be end

this scarecrow of a suit have in course of time become so complicated that no man alive know what it mean

chapter ii in fashion it be but a glimpse of the world of fashion that we want on this same miry afternoon
it be not so unlike the court of chancery but that we may pass from the one scene to the other as the crow fly

sir leicester dedlock be only a baronet but there be no mighty baronet than he
his family be as old as the hill and infinitely more respectable
he have a general opinion that the world might get on without hill but would be do up without dedlock
he would on the whole admit nature to be a good idea a little low perhaps when not enclose with a park-fence but an idea dependent for its execution on your great county family
he be a gentleman of strict conscience disdainful of all littleness and meanness and ready on the short notice to die any death you may please to mention rather than give occasion for the least impeachment of his integrity
he be an honourable obstinate truthful high-spirit intensely prejudiced perfectly unreasonable man
sir leicester be twenty year full measure old than my lady
he will never see sixty-five again nor perhaps sixty-six nor yet sixty-seven
he have a twist of the gout now and then and walk a little stiffly
he be of a worthy presence with his light-grey hair and whisker his fine shirt-frill his pure-white waistcoat and his blue coat with bright button always button
he be ceremonious stately most polite on every occasion to my lady and hold her personal attraction in the high estimation

how alexander weep when he have no more world to conquer everybody know--or have some reason to know by this time the matter have be rather frequently mention
my lady dedlock have conquer her world fall not into the melting but rather into the freezing mood
an exhausted composure a wear-out placidity an equanimity of fatigue not to be ruffle by interest or satisfaction be the trophy of her victory
she be perfectly well-breed

she have beauty still and if it be not in its heyday it be not yet in its autumn

i be bring up from my early remembrance--like some of the princess in the fairy story only i be not charming--by my godmother
at least i only know she as such
she be a good good woman
she go to church three time every sunday and to morning prayer on wednesday and fridays and to lecture whenever there be lecture and never miss
she be handsome and if she have ever smile would have be i use to think like an angel--but she never smile
she be always grave and strict
she be so very good herself i think that the badness of other people make she frown all her life
i feel so different from she even make every allowance for the difference between a child and a woman i feel so poor so trifling and so far off that i never could be unrestrained with she--no could never even love she as i wish
it make i very sorry to consider how good she be and how unworthy of she i be and i use ardently to hope that i might have a well heart and i talk it over very often with the dear old doll but i never love my godmother as i ought to have love she and as i feel i must have love she if i have be a well girl
this make i i dare say more timid and retire than i naturally be and cast i upon dolly as the only friend with whom i feel at ease

i never go out at all
it be my birthday
there be holiday at school on other birthday--none on mine
there be rejoicing at home on other birthday as i know from what i hear the girl relate to one another--there be none on mine
my birthday be the most melancholy day at home in the whole year
i have mention that unless my vanity should deceive i as i know it may for i may be very vain without suspect it though indeed i donot my comprehension be quicken when my affection be

chapter vii the ghost s walk while esther sleep and while esther wake it be still wet weather down at the place in lincolnshire
the rain be ever fall--drip drip drip--by day and night upon the broad flagged terrace- pavement the ghost s walk
the weather be so very bad down in lincolnshire that the lively imagination can scarcely apprehend its ever be fine again
not that there be any superabundant life of imagination on the spot for sir leicester be not here and truly even if he be would not do much for it in that particular but be in paris with my lady and solitude with dusky wing sit brood upon chesney wold
there may be some motion of fancy among the low animal at chesney wold
the horse in the stable--the long stable in a barren red-brick court-yard where there be a great bell in a turret and a clock with a large face which the pigeon who live near it and who love to perch upon its shoulder seem to be always consult--they may contemplate some mental picture of fine weather on occasion and may be well artist at they than the groom
the old roan so famous for cross-country work turn his large eyeball to the grated window near his rack may remember the fresh leaf that glisten there at other time and the scent that stream in and may have a fine run with the hound while the human helper clear out the next stall never stir beyond his pitchfork and birch-broom
the grey whose place be opposite the door and who with an impatient rattle of his halter prick his ear and turn his head so wistfully when it be open and to whom the opener say woa grey then steady
noabody want you to-day may know it quite as well as the man
the whole seemingly monotonous and uncompanionable half-dozen stable together may pass the long wet hour when the door be shut in lively communication than be hold in the servant hall or at the dedlock arm or may even beguile the time by improve perhaps corrupting the pony in the loose-box in the corner
so the mastiff doze in his kennel in the court-yard with his large head on his paw may think of the hot sunshine when the shadow of the stable-building tire his patience out by change and leave he at one time of the day no broad refuge than the shadow of his own house where he sit on end pant and growling short and very much want something to worry besides himself and his chain
so now half-waking and all-wink he may recall the house full of company the coach-house full of vehicle the stable full of horse and the out-building full of attendant upon horse until he be undecided about the present and come forth to see how it be
then with that impatient shake of himself he may growl in the spirit rain rain rain
nothing but rain--and no family here as he go in again and lie down with a gloomy yawn
so with the dog in the kennel-building across the park who have their restless fit and whose doleful voice when the wind have be very obstinate have even make it know in the house itself-- upstairs downstair and in my lady s chamber
they may hunt the whole country-side while the raindrop be patter round their inactivity
so the rabbit with their self-betray tail frisk in and out of hole at root of tree may be lively with idea of the breezy day when their ear be blow about or of those season of interest when there be sweet young plant to gnaw
the turkey in the poultry-yard always trouble with a class-grievance probably christmas may be reminiscent of that summer morning wrongfully take from he when he get into the lane among the felled tree where there be a barn and barley
the discontent goose who stoop to pass under the old gateway twenty foot high may gabble out if we only know it a waddle preference for weather when the gateway cast its shadow on the ground
be this as it may there be not much fancy otherwise stir at chesney wold
if there be a little at any odd moment it go like a little noise in that old echo place a long way and usually lead off to ghost and mystery

equity send question to law law send question back to equity law find it cannot do this equity find it cannot do that neither can so much as say it cannot do anything without this solicitor instructing and this counsel appear for a and that solicitor instructing and that counsel appear for b and so on through the whole alphabet like the history of the apple pie
and thus through year and year and life and life everything go on constantly begin over and over again and nothing ever end
and we cannot get out of the suit on any term for we be make party to it and must be party to it whether we like it or not

chapter x the law-writer on the eastern border of chancery lane that be to say more particularly in cook s court cursitor street mr

in the shade of cook s court at most time a shady place mr

in his lifetime and likewise in the period of snagsby s time of seven long year there dwelt with peffer in the same law- stationering premise a niece--a short shrewd niece something too violently compress about the waist and with a sharp nose like a sharp autumn evening incline to be frosty towards the end
the cook s courtiers have a rumour fly among they that the mother of this niece do in her daughter s childhood move by too jealous a solicitude that her figure should approach perfection lace she up every morning with her maternal foot against the bed-post for a strong hold and purchase and far that she exhibit internally pint of vinegar and lemon-juice which acid they hold have mount to the nose and temper of the patient

dandyism
there be no king george the fourth now more the pity to set the dandy fashion there be no clear-starch jack-towel neckcloth no short-waiste coat no false calf no stay
there be no caricature now of effeminate exquisite so arrayed swoon in opera box with excess of delight and be revive by other dainty creature poke long-neck scent-bottle at their nose
there be no beau whom it take four man at once to shake into his buckskin or who go to see all the execution or who be trouble with the self-reproach of have once consume a pea
but be there dandyism in the brilliant and distinguished circle notwithstanding dandyism of a more mischievous sort that have get below the surface and be do less harmless thing than jack- towel itself and stop its own digestion to which no rational person need particularly object
why yes
it cannot be disguise
there be at chesney wold this january week some lady and gentleman of the new fashion who have set up a dandyism--in religion for instance
who in mere lackadaisical want of an emotion have agree upon a little dandy talk about the vulgar want faith in thing in general mean in the thing that have be try and find wanting as though a low fellow should unaccountably lose faith in a bad shilling after find it out
who would make the vulgar very picturesque and faithful by put back the hand upon the clock of time and cancel a few hundred year of history
there be also lady and gentleman of another fashion not so new but very elegant who have agree to put a smooth glaze on the world and to keep down all its reality
for whom everything must be languid and pretty
who have find out the perpetual stoppage
who be to rejoice at nothing and be sorry for nothing
who be not to be disturb by idea
on whom even the fine art attend in powder and walk backward like the lord chamberlain must array themselves in the milliner and tailor pattern of past generation and be particularly careful not to be in earnest or to receive any impress from the move age

chapter xvi tom-all-alone s my lady dedlock be restless very restless
the astonished fashionable intelligence hardly know where to have she
to-day she be at chesney wold yesterday she be at her house in town to- morrow she may be abroad for anything the fashionable intelligence can with confidence predict
even sir leicester s gallantry have some trouble to keep pace with she
it would have more but that his other faithful ally for well and for bad--the gout--dart into the old oak bed-chamber at chesney wold and grip he by both leg
sir leicester receive the gout as a troublesome demon but still a demon of the patrician order
all the dedlock in the direct male line through a course of time during and beyond which the memory of man goeth not to the contrary have have the gout
it can be prove sir
other man s father may have die of the rheumatism or may have take base contagion from the tainted blood of the sick vulgar but the dedlock family have communicate something exclusive even to the levelling process of die by die of their own family gout
it have come down through the illustrious line like the plate or the picture or the place in lincolnshire
it be among their dignity
sir leicester be perhaps not wholly without an impression though he have never resolve it into word that the angel of death in the discharge of his necessary duty may observe to the shade of the aristocracy my lord and gentleman i have the honour to present to you another dedlock certify to have arrive per the family gout hence sir leicester yield up his family leg to the family disorder as if he hold his name and fortune on that feudal tenure
he feel that for a dedlock to be lay upon his back and spasmodically twitch and stab in his extremity be a liberty take somewhere but he think we have all yield to this it belong to we it have for some hundred of year be understand that we be not to make the vault in the park interesting on more ignoble term and i submit myself to the compromise and a goodly show he make lie in a flush of crimson and gold in the midst of the great drawing-room before his favourite picture of my lady with broad strip of sunlight shin in down the long perspective through the long line of window and alternate with soft relief of shadow
outside the stately oak root for age in the green ground which have never know ploughshare but be still a chase when king ride to battle with sword and shield and ride a-hunting with bow and arrow bear witness to his greatness
inside his forefather look on he from the wall say each of we be a pass reality here and leave this coloured shadow of himself and melt into remembrance as dreamy as the distant voice of the rook now lull you to rest and hear their testimony to his greatness too
and he be very great this day
and woe to boythorn or other daring wight who shall presumptuously contest an inch with he
my lady be at present represent near sir leicester by her portrait
she have flit away to town with no intention of remain there and will soon flit hither again to the confusion of the fashionable intelligence
the house in town be not prepare for her reception
it be muffled and dreary
only one mercury in powder gape disconsolate at the hall-window and he mention last night to another mercury of his acquaintance also accustom to good society that if that sort of thing be to last--which it couldnot for a man of his spirit couldnot bear it and a man of his figure couldnot be expect to bear it--there would be no resource for he upon his honour but to cut his throat
what connexion can there be between the place in lincolnshire the house in town the mercury in powder and the whereabout of jo the outlaw with the broom who have that distant ray of light upon he when he sweep the churchyard-step
what connexion can there have be between many people in the innumerable history of this world who from opposite side of great gulfs have nevertheless be very curiously bring together
jo sweep his crossing all day long unconscious of the link if any link there be
he sum up his mental condition when ask a question by reply that he donot know nothink he know that itbe hard to keep the mud off the crossing in dirty weather and hard still to live by do it
nobody teach he even that much he find it out
jo live--that be to say jo have not yet die--in a ruinous place know to the like of he by the name of tom-all-alone s
it be a black dilapidated street avoid by all decent people where the crazy house be seize upon when their decay be far advanced by some bold vagrant who after establish their own possession take to let they out in lodging
now these tumble tenement contain by night a swarm of misery
as on the ruin human wretch vermin parasite appear so these ruin shelter have breed a crowd of foul existence that crawl in and out of gap in wall and board and coil itself to sleep in maggot number where the rain drip in and come and go fetch and carry fever and sow more evil in its every footprint than lord coodle and sir thomas doodle and the duke of foodle and all the fine gentleman in office down to zoodle shall set right in five hundred year--though bear expressly to do it
twice lately there have be a crash and a cloud of dust like the springing of a mine in tom-all-alone s and each time a house have fall
these accident have make a paragraph in the newspaper and have fill a bed or two in the near hospital
the gap remain and there be not unpopular lodging among the rubbish
as several more house be nearly ready to go the next crash in tom- all-alone s may be expect to be a good one
this desirable property be in chancery of course
it would be an insult to the discernment of any man with half an eye to tell he so
whether tom be the popular representative of the original plaintiff or defendant in jarndyce and jarndyce or whether tom live here when the suit have lay the street waste all alone until other settler come to join he or whether the traditional title be a comprehensive name for a retreat cut off from honest company and put out of the pale of hope perhaps nobody know
certainly jo donot know
for i donot say jo i donot know nothink it must be a strange state to be like jo
to shuffle through the street unfamiliar with the shape and in utter darkness as to the meaning of those mysterious symbol so abundant over the shop and at the corner of street and on the door and in the window
to see people read and to see people write and to see the postman deliver letter and not to have the least idea of all that language--to be to every scrap of it stone blind and dumb
it must be very puzzling to see the good company go to the church on sundays with their book in their hand and to think for perhaps jo do think at odd time what do it all mean and if it mean anything to anybody how come it that it mean nothing to i
to be hustle and jostle and move on and really to feel that it would appear to be perfectly true that i have no business here or there or anywhere and yet to be perplex by the consideration that i be here somehow too and everybody overlook i until i become the creature that i be
it must be a strange state not merely to be tell that i be scarcely human as in the case of my offer myself for a witness but to feel it of my own knowledge all my life
to see the horse dog and cattle go by i and to know that in ignorance i belong to they and not to the superior being in my shape whose delicacy i offend
jo s idea of a criminal trial or a judge or a bishop or a government or that inestimable jewel to he if he only know it the constitution should be strange
his whole material and immaterial life be wonderfully strange his death the strange thing of all
jo come out of tom-all-alone s meet the tardy morning which be always late in get down there and munch his dirty bit of bread as he come along
his way lie through many street and the house not yet be open he sit down to breakfast on the door-step of the society for the propagation of the gospel in foreign parts and give it a brush when he have finish as an acknowledgment of the accommodation
he admire the size of the edifice and wonder what itbe all about
he have no idea poor wretch of the spiritual destitution of a coral reef in the pacific or what it cost to look up the precious soul among the coco-nut and bread-fruit
he go to his crossing and begin to lay it out for the day
the town awake the great tee-totum be set up for its daily spin and whirl all that unaccountable reading and writing which have be suspend for a few hour recommence
jo and the other low animal get on in the unintelligible mess as they can
it be market-day
the blinded oxen over-goad over-drive never guide run into wrong place and be beat out and plunge red- eyed and foam at stone wall and often sorely hurt the innocent and often sorely hurt themselves
very like jo and his order very very like
a band of music come and play
jo listen to it
so do a dog a drover s dog wait for his master outside a butcher s shop and evidently think about those sheep he have have upon his mind for some hour and be happily rid of
he seem perplexed respect three or four cannot remember where he leave they look up and down the street as half expect to see they astray suddenly prick up his ear and remember all about it
a thoroughly vagabond dog accustom to low company and public- house a terrific dog to sheep ready at a whistle to scamper over their back and tear out mouthful of their wool but an educate improve develop dog who have be teach his duty and know how to discharge they
he and jo listen to the music probably with much the same amount of animal satisfaction likewise as to awakened association aspiration or regret melancholy or joyful reference to thing beyond the sens they be probably upon a par
but otherwise how far above the human listener be the brute
turn that dog s descendant wild like jo and in a very few year they will so degenerate that they will lose even their bark--but not their bite
the day change as it wear itself away and become dark and drizzly

the learn gentleman who be always so tremendously indignant at the unprecedented outrage commit on the feeling of his client by the opposite party that he never seem likely to recover it be do infinitely well than might be expect in switzerland

perkins ma am and donot you be surprised lord bless you if that young man come in at last for old krook s money chapter xxi the smallweed family in a rather ill-favour and ill-savoured neighbourhood though one of its rise ground bear the name of mount pleasant the elfin smallweed christen bartholomew and know on the domestic hearth as bart pass that limited portion of his time on which the office and its contingency have no claim
he dwell in a little narrow street always solitary shady and sad closely bricke in on all side like a tomb but where there yet linger the stump of an old forest tree whose flavour be about as fresh and natural as the smallweed smack of youth
there have be only one child in the smallweed family for several generation
little old man and woman there have be but no child until mr
smallweed s grandmother now live become weak in her intellect and fall for the first time into a childish state
with such infantine grace as a total want of observation memory understanding and interest and an eternal disposition to fall asleep over the fire and into it mr
smallweed s grandmother have undoubtedly brighten the family
mr
smallweed s grandfather be likewise of the party
he be in a helpless condition as to his low and nearly so as to his upper limb but his mind be unimpaired
it hold as well as it ever hold the first four rule of arithmetic and a certain small collection of the hard fact
in respect of ideality reverence wonder and other such phrenological attribute it be no bad off than it use to be
everything that mr
smallweed s grandfather ever put away in his mind be a grub at first and be a grub at last
in all his life he have never breed a single butterfly
the father of this pleasant grandfather of the neighbourhood of mount pleasant be a horny-skin two-legged money-get specie of spider who spin webs to catch unwary fly and retire into hole until they be entrap
the name of this old pagan s god be compound interest
he live for it marry it die of it
meet with a heavy loss in an honest little enterprise in which all the loss be intend to have be on the other side he break something--something necessary to his existence therefore it couldnot have be his heart--and make an end of his career
as his character be not good and he have be breed at a charity school in a complete course accord to question and answer of those ancient people the amorites and hittites he be frequently quote as an example of the failure of education
his spirit shine through his son to whom he have always preach of go out early in life and whom he make a clerk in a sharp scrivener s office at twelve year old
there the young gentleman improve his mind which be of a lean and anxious character and develop the family gift gradually elevate himself into the discount profession
go out early in life and marry late as his father have do before he he too begat a lean and anxious- minded son who in his turn go out early in life and marrying late become the father of bartholomew and judith smallweed twin
during the whole time consume in the slow growth of this family tree the house of smallweed always early to go out and late to marry have strengthen itself in its practical character have discard all amusement discountenance all story-book fairy- tale fiction and fable and banish all levity whatsoever
hence the gratifying fact that it have have no child bear to it and that the complete little man and woman whom it have produce have be observe to bear a likeness to old monkey with something depress on their mind
at the present time in the dark little parlour certain foot below the level of the street--a grim hard uncouth parlour only ornament with the coarse of baize table-cover and the hard of sheet-iron tea-tray and offer in its decorative character no bad allegorical representation of grandfather smallweed s mind-- seat in two black horsehair porter s chair one on each side of the fire-place the superannuate mr
and mrs
smallweed while away the rosy hour
on the stove be a couple of trivet for the pot and kettle which it be grandfather smallweed s usual occupation to watch and project from the chimney-piece between they be a sort of brass gallow for roasting which he also superintend when it be in action
under the venerable mr
smallweed s seat and guard by his spindle leg be a drawer in his chair report to contain property to a fabulous amount
beside he be a spare cushion with which he be always provide in order that he may have something to throw at the venerable partner of his respected age whenever she make an allusion to money--a subject on which he be particularly sensitive

smallweed the young that the two knead into one would hardly make a young person of average proportion while she so happily exemplify the before-mention family likeness to the monkey tribe that attire in a spangled robe and cap she might walk about the table-land on the top of a barrel- organ without exciting much remark as an unusual speciman

chapter xxii mr
bucket allegory look pretty cool in lincoln s inn fields though the evening be hot for both mr
tulkinghorn s window be wide open and the room be lofty gusty and gloomy
these may not be desirable characteristic when november come with fog and sleet or january with ice and snow but they have their merit in the sultry long vacation weather
they enable allegory though it have cheek like peach and knee like bunch of blossom and rosy swelling for calf to its leg and muscle to its arm to look tolerably cool to-night
plenty of dust come in at mr
tulkinghorn s window and plenty more have generate among his furniture and paper
it lie thick everywhere
when a breeze from the country that have lose its way take fright and make a blind hurry to rush out again it fling as much dust in the eye of allegory as the law--or mr
tulkinghorn one of its trusty representative--may scatter on occasion in the eye of the laity
in his lower magazine of dust the universal article into which his paper and himself and all his client and all thing of earth animate and inanimate be resolve mr
tulkinghorn sit at one of the open window enjoy a bottle of old port
though a hard-grain man close dry and silent he can enjoy old wine with the good
he have a priceless bin of port in some artful cellar under the fields which be one of his many secret
when he din alone in chamber as he have din to-day and have his bit of fish and his steak or chicken bring in from the coffee-house he descend with a candle to the echo region below the deserted mansion and herald by a remote reverberation of thunder door come gravely back encircled by an earthy atmosphere and carry a bottle from which he pour a radiant nectar two score and ten year old that blush in the glass to find itself so famous and fill the whole room with the fragrance of southern grape
mr
tulkinghorn sit in the twilight by the open window enjoy his wine
as if it whisper to he of its fifty year of silence and seclusion it shut he up the close
more impenetrable than ever he sit and drink and mellow as it be in secrecy ponder at that twilight hour on all the mystery he know associate with darken wood in the country and vast blank shut-up house in town and perhaps spar a thought or two for himself and his family history and his money and his will--all a mystery to every one--and that one bachelor friend of his a man of the same mould and a lawyer too who live the same kind of life until he be seventy-five year old and then suddenly conceive as it be suppose an impression that it be too monotonous give his gold watch to his hair-dresser one summer evening and walk leisurely home to the temple and hang himself
but mr
tulkinghorn be not alone to-night to ponder at his usual length
seat at the same table though with his chair modestly and uncomfortably draw a little way from it sit a bald mild shin man who cough respectfully behind his hand when the lawyer bid he fill his glass
now snagsby say mr
tulkinghorn to go over this odd story again if you please sir you tell i when you be so good as to step round here last night-- for which i must ask you to excuse i if it be a liberty sir but i remember that you have take a sort of an interest in that person and i think it possible that you might--just--wish--to-- mr
tulkinghorn be not the man to help he to any conclusion or to admit anything as to any possibility concern himself
so mr

chapter xxiii esther s narrative we come home from mr
boythorn s after six pleasant week
we be often in the park and in the wood and seldom pass the lodge where we have take shelter without look in to speak to the keeper s wife but we see no more of lady dedlock except at church on sundays
there be company at chesney wold and although several beautiful face surround she her face retain the same influence on i as at first
i do not quite know even now whether it be painful or pleasurable whether it draw i towards she or make i shrink from she
i think i admire she with a kind of fear and i know that in her presence my thought always wander back as they have do at first to that old time of my life
i have a fancy on more than one of these sundays that what this lady so curiously be to i i be to she--i mean that i disturb her thought as she influence mine though in some different way
but when i steal a glance at she and see she so composed and distant and unapproachable i feel this to be a foolish weakness
indeed i feel the whole state of my mind in reference to she to be weak and unreasonable and i remonstrate with myself about it as much as i could
one incident that occur before we quit mr
boythorn s house i have well mention in this place
i be walk in the garden with ada and when i be tell that some one wish to see i
go into the breakfast-room where this person be wait i find it to be the french maid who have cast off her shoe and walk through the wet grass on the day when it thunder and lighten
mademoiselle she begin look fixedly at i with her too-eager eye though otherwise present an agreeable appearance and speak neither with boldness nor servility i have take a great liberty in come here but you know how to excuse it be so amiable mademoiselle no excuse be necessary i return if you wish to speak to i that be my desire mademoiselle
a thousand thank for the permission
i have your leave to speak
be it not she say in a quick natural way
certainly say i
mademoiselle you be so amiable
listen then if you please
i have leave my lady
we could not agree
my lady be so high so very high
pardon

but i say she be so high so very high
i will not say a word more
all the world know that go on if you please say i

you be good accomplish and beautiful as an angel
ah could i have the honour of be your domestic i be sorry-- i begin
do not dismiss i so soon mademoiselle she say with an involuntary contraction of her fine black eyebrow

well

well

at that time and for a good many week after that time richard be constant in his visit
besides come every saturday or sunday and remain with we until monday morning he sometimes ride out on horseback unexpectedly and pass the evening with we and ride back again early next day
he be as vivacious as ever and tell we he be very industrious but i be not easy in my mind about he
it appear to i that his industry be all misdirect
i could not find that it lead to anything but the formation of delusive hope in connexion with the suit already the pernicious cause of so much sorrow and ruin
he have get at the core of that mystery now he tell we and nothing could be plainer than that the will under which he and ada be to take i donot know how many thousand of pound must be finally establish if there be any sense or justice in the court of chancery--but oh what a great if that sound in my ear--and that this happy conclusion could not be much long delay
he prove this to himself by all the weary argument on that side he have read and every one of they sink he deeply in the infatuation
he have even begin to haunt the court
he tell we how he see miss flite there daily how they talk together and how he do her little kindness and how while he laugh at she he pity she from his heart
but he never think--never my poor dear sanguine richard capable of so much happiness then and with such well thing before him-- what a fatal link be riveting between his fresh youth and her fade age between his free hope and her cage bird and her hungry garret and her wander mind
ada love he too well to mistrust he much in anything he say or do and my guardian though he frequently complain of the east wind and read more than usual in the growlery preserve a strict silence on the subject
so i think one day when i go to london to meet caddy jellyby at her solicitation i would ask richard to be in wait for i at the coach-office that we might have a little talk together
i find he there when i arrive and we walk away arm in arm
well richard say i as soon as i could begin to be grave with he be you begin to feel more settle now oh yes my dear return richard
ibe all right enough but settle say i

well
perhaps itbe not
settle

when i say this business of course i mean the-- forbid subject do you think it will ever be in a settle state say i
not the least doubt of it answer richard
we walk a little way without speak and presently richard address i in his frank and most feeling manner thus my dear esther i understand you and i wish to heaven i be a more constant sort of fellow
i donot mean constant to ada for i love she dearly--well and well every day--but constant to myself
somehow i mean something that i cannot very well express but youwill make it out if i be a more constant sort of fellow i should have hold on either to badger or to kenge and carboy like grim death and should have begin to be steady and systematic by this time and shouldnot be in debt and-- be you in debt richard yes say richard i be a little so my dear

my dear esther i be a very unfortunate dog not to be more settle but how can i be more settle
if you live in an unfinished house you couldnot settle down in it if you be condemn to leave everything you undertake unfinished you would find it hard to apply yourself to anything and yet thatbe my unhappy case
i be bear into this unfinished contention with all its chance and change and it begin to unsettle i before i quite know the difference between a suit at law and a suit of clothe and it have go on unsettle i ever since and here i be now conscious sometimes that i be but a worthless fellow to love my confiding cousin ada we be in a solitary place and he put his hand before his eye and sob as he say the word

chapter xxvi sharpshooters wintry morning look with dull eye and sallow face upon the neighbourhood of leicester square find its inhabitant unwilling to get out of bed
many of they be not early riser at the bright of time be bird of night who roost when the sun be high and be wide awake and keen for prey when the star shine out
behind dingy blind and curtain in upper story and garret skulk more or less under false name false hair false title false jewellery and false history a colony of brigand lie in their first sleep
gentleman of the green-baize road who could discourse from personal experience of foreign galley and home treadmill spy of strong government that eternally quake with weakness and miserable fear break traitor coward bully gamester shuffler swindler and false witness some not unmarke by the branding-iron beneath their dirty braid all with more cruelty in they than be in nero and more crime than be in newgate
for howsoever bad the devil can be in fustian or smock-frock and he can be very bad in both he be a more designing callous and intolerable devil when he stick a pin in his shirt-front call himself a gentleman back a card or colour play a game or so of billiard and know a little about bill and promissory note than in any other form he wear
and in such form mr

miss volumnia display in early life a pretty talent for cut ornament out of coloured paper and also for singing to the guitar in the spanish tongue and propound french conundrum in country house pass the twenty year of her existence between twenty and forty in a sufficiently agreeable manner

i believe that nothing belong to the family which it have be possible to break be unbroken at the time of those preparation for caddy s marriage that nothing which it have be possible to spoil in any way be unspoilt and that no domestic object which be capable of collect dirt from a dear child s knee to the door-plate be without as much dirt as could well accumulate upon it

chapter xxxv esther s narrative i lay ill through several week and the usual tenor of my life become like an old remembrance
but this be not the effect of time so much as of the change in all my habit make by the helplessness and inaction of a sick-room
before i have be confine to it many day everything else seem to have retire into a remote distance where there be little or no separation between the various stage of my life which have be really divide by year
in fall ill i seem to have cross a dark lake and to have leave all my experience mingle together by the great distance on the healthy shore
my housekeeping duty though at first it cause i great anxiety to think that they be unperforme be soon as far off as the old of the old duty at greenleaf or the summer afternoon when i go home from school with my portfolio under my arm and my childish shadow at my side to my godmother s house
i have never know before how short life really be and into how small a space the mind could put it
while i be very ill the way in which these division of time become confused with one another distress my mind exceedingly
at once a child an eld girl and the little woman i have be so happy as i be not only oppress by care and difficulty adapt to each station but by the great perplexity of endlessly try to reconcile they
i suppose that few who have not be in such a condition can quite understand what i mean or what painful unrest arise from this source
for the same reason i be almost afraid to hint at that time in my disorder--it seem one long night but i believe there be both night and day in it--when i labour up colossal staircase ever strive to reach the top and ever turn as i have see a worm in a garden path by some obstruction and labour again
i know perfectly at interval and i think vaguely at most time that i be in my bed and i talk with charley and feel her touch and know she very well yet i would find myself complain oh more of these never-end stair charley--more and more--pile up to the sky i think and labour on again
dare i hint at that bad time when string together somewhere in great black space there be a flaming necklace or ring or starry circle of some kind of which i be one of the bead
and when my only prayer be to be take off from the rest and when it be such inexplicable agony and misery to be a part of the dreadful thing
perhaps the less i say of these sick experience the less tedious and the more intelligible i shall be
i do not recall they to make other unhappy or because i be now the least unhappy in remember they
it may be that if we know more of such strange affliction we might be the well able to alleviate their intensity
the repose that succeed the long delicious sleep the blissful rest when in my weakness i be too calm to have any care for myself and could have hear or so i think now that i be die with no other emotion than with a pitying love for those i leave behind--this state can be perhaps more widely understood
i be in this state when i first shrink from the light as it twinkle on i once more and know with a boundless joy for which no word be rapturous enough that i should see again

he walk into chesney wold as if it be next door to his chamber and return to his chamber as if he have never be out of lincoln s inn fields
he neither change his dress before the journey nor talk of it afterwards
he melt out of his turret-room this morning just as now in the late twilight he melt into his own square

chapter xlvii jo s will as allan woodcourt and jo procee along the street where the high church spire and the distance be so near and clear in the morning light that the city itself seem renew by rest allan revolve in his mind how and where he shall bestow his companion

chapter l esther s narrative it happen that when i come home from deal i find a note from caddy jellyby as we always continue to call she inform i that her health which have be for some time very delicate be bad and that she would be more glad than she could tell i if i would go to see she
it be a note of a few line write from the couch on which she lie and enclose to i in another from her husband in which he second her entreaty with much solicitude
caddy be now the mother and i the godmother of such a poor little baby--such a tiny old-faced mite with a countenance that seem to be scarcely anything but cap-border and a little lean long-finger hand always clench under its chin
it would lie in this attitude all day with its bright speck of eye open wonder as i use to imagine how it come to be so small and weak
whenever it be move it cry but at all other time it be so patient that the sole desire of its life appear to be to lie quiet and think
it have curious little dark vein in its face and curious little dark mark under its eye like faint remembrance of poor caddy s inky day and altogether to those who be not use to it it be quite a piteous little sight
but it be enough for caddy that she be use to it
the project with which she beguile her illness for little esther s education and little esther s marriage and even for her own old age as the grandmother of little esther s little esther be so prettily expressive of devotion to this pride of her life that i should be tempt to recall some of they but for the timely remembrance that i be get on irregularly as it be
to return to the letter
caddy have a superstition about i which have be strengthen in her mind ever since that night long ago when she have lain asleep with her head in my lap
she almost--i think i must say quite--believe that i do she good whenever i be near she
now although this be such a fancy of the affectionate girl s that i be almost ashamed to mention it still it might have all the force of a fact when she be really ill
therefore i set off to caddy with my guardian s consent post-haste and she and prince make so much of i that there never be anything like it
next day i go again to sit with she and next day i go again
it be a very easy journey for i have only to rise a little early in the morning and keep my account and attend to housekeeping matter before leave home
but when i have make these three visit my guardian say to i on my return at night now little woman little woman this will never do
constant dropping will wear away a stone and constant coaching will wear out a dame durden
we will go to london for a while and take possession of our old lodging not for i dear guardian say i for i never feel tired which be strictly true
i be only too happy to be in such request
for i then return my guardian or for ada or for both of we
it be somebody s birthday to-morrow i think truly i think it be say i kiss my darling who would be twenty-one to-morrow
well observe my guardian half pleasantly half seriously thatbe a great occasion and will give my fair cousin some necessary business to transact in assertion of her independence and will make london a more convenient place for all of we
so to london we will go
that be settle there be another thing--how have you leave caddy very unwell guardian
i fear it will be some time before she regain her health and strength what do you call some time now ask my guardian thoughtfully
some week i be afraid ah he begin to walk about the room with his hand in his pocket show that he have be think as much
now what do you say about her doctor
be he a good doctor my love i feel oblige to confess that i know nothing to the contrary but that prince and i have agree only that evening that we would like his opinion to be confirm by some one
well you know return my guardian quickly therebe woodcourt i have not mean that and be rather take by surprise

chapter lv flight inspector bucket of the detective have not yet strike his great blow as just now chronicle but be yet refresh himself with sleep preparatory to his field-day when through the night and along the freezing wintry road a chaise and pair come out of lincolnshire make its way towards london
railroad shall soon traverse all this country and with a rattle and a glare the engine and train shall shoot like a meteor over the wide night-landscape turn the moon paler but as yet such thing be non-existent in these part though not wholly unexpected

bridge be begin and their not yet united pier desolately look at one another over road and stream like brick and mortar couple with an obstacle to their union fragment of embankment be throw up and leave as precipice with torrent of rusty cart and barrow tumble over they tripod of tall pole appear on hilltop where there be rumour of tunnel everything look chaotic and abandon in full hopelessness

chapter lvii esther s narrative i have go to bed and fall asleep when my guardian knock at the door of my room and beg i to get up directly
on my hurrying to speak to he and learn what have happen he tell i after a word or two of preparation that there have be a discovery at sir leicester dedlock s
that my mother have flee that a person be now at our door who be empower to convey to she the full assurance of affectionate protection and forgiveness if he could possibly find she and that i be seek for to accompany he in the hope that my entreaty might prevail upon she if his fail
something to this general purpose i make out but i be throw into such a tumult of alarm and hurry and distress that in spite of every effort i could make to subdue my agitation i do not seem to myself fully to recover my right mind until hour have pass
but i dress and wrap up expeditiously without wake charley or any one and go down to mr
bucket who be the person entrust with the secret
in take i to he my guardian tell i this and also explain how it be that he have come to think of i
mr

his manner be very keen and yet considerate when he explain to i that a great deal might depend on my be able to answer without confusion a few question that he wish to ask i
these be chiefly whether i have have much communication with my mother to whom he only refer as lady dedlock when and where i have speak with her last and how she have become possess of my handkerchief
when i have satisfy he on these point he ask i particularly to consider--take time to think--whether within my knowledge there be any one no matter where in whom she might be at all likely to confide under circumstance of the last necessity
i could think of no one but my guardian

he now tell he to go on again and say to i after consider within himself for a few moment that he have make up his mind how to proceed

it be now past one as i see by the clock against the wall

i be far from sure that i be not in a dream
we rattle with great rapidity through such a labyrinth of street that i soon lose all idea where we be except that we have cross and re-cross the river and still seem to be traverse a low-lie waterside dense neighbourhood of narrow thoroughfare chequer by dock and basin high pile of warehouse swing-bridge and mast of ship
at length we stop at the corner of a little slimy turning which the wind from the river rush up it do not purify and i see my companion by the light of his lantern in conference with several man who look like a mixture of police and sailor
against the mouldering wall by which they stand there be a bill on which i could discern the word find drown and this and an inscription about drag possess i with the awful suspicion shadow forth in our visit to that place
i have no need to remind myself that i be not there by the indulgence of any feeling of mine to increase the difficulty of the search or to lessen its hope or enhance its delay
i remain quiet but what i suffer in that dreadful spot i never can forget
and still it be like the horror of a dream
a man yet dark and muddy in long swollen sodden boot and a hat like they be call out of a boat and whisper with mr
bucket who go away with he down some slippery step--as if to look at something secret that he have to show
they come back wipe their hand upon their coat after turn over something wet but thank god it be not what i fear

he stand up to look over the parapet he alight and go back after a shadowy female figure that flit past we and he gaze into the profound black pit of water with a face that make my heart die within i
the river have a fearful look so overcast and secret creep away so fast between the low flat line of shore--so heavy with indistinct and awful shape both of substance and shadow so death-like and mysterious
i have see it many time since then by sunlight and by moonlight but never free from the impression of that journey

volumnia in her room up a retired landing on the staircase--the second turning past the end of the carving and gilding a cousinly room contain a fearful abortion of a portrait of sir leicester banish for its crime and command in the day a solemn yard plant with dry-up shrub like antediluvian specimen of black tea--be a prey to horror of many kind
not last nor least among they possibly be a horror of what may befall her little income in the event as she express it of anything happen to sir leicester
anything in this sense mean one thing only and that the last thing that can happen to the consciousness of any baronet in the known world
an effect of these horror be that volumnia find she cannot go to bed in her own room or sit by the fire in her own room but must come forth with her fair head tie up in a profusion of shawl and her fair form enrobe in drapery and parade the mansion like a ghost particularly haunt the room warm and luxurious prepare for one who still do not return

chapter lxvi down in lincolnshire there be a hush upon chesney wold in these altered day as there be upon a portion of the family history
the story go that sir leicester pay some who could have speak out to hold their peace but it be a lame story feebly whisper and creep about and any bright spark of life it show soon die away
it be know for certain that the handsome lady dedlock lie in the mausoleum in the park where the tree arch darkly overhead and the owl be hear at night make the wood ring but whence she be bring home to be lay among the echo of that solitary place or how she die be all mystery
some of her old friend principally to be find among the peachy-cheek charmer with the skeleton throat do once occasionally say as they toy in a ghastly manner with large fan--like charmer reduce to flirt with grim death after lose all their other beaux--do once occasionally say when the world assemble together that they wonder the ash of the dedlocks entomb in the mausoleum never rise against the profanation of her company
but the dead-and-go dedlock take it very calmly and have never be know to object
up from among the fern in the hollow and wind by the bridle- road among the tree come sometimes to this lonely spot the sound of horse hoof
then may be see sir leicester--invalid bent and almost blind but of worthy presence yet--rid with a stalwart man beside he constant to his bridle-rein
when they come to a certain spot before the mausoleum-door sir leicester s accustomed horse stop of his own accord and sir leicester pull off his hat be still for a few moment before they ride away
war rage yet with the audacious boythorn though at uncertain interval and now hotly and now coolly flicker like an unsteady fire
the truth be say to be that when sir leicester come down to lincolnshire for good mr
boythorn show a manifest desire to abandon his right of way and do whatever sir leicester would which sir leicester conceive to be a condescension to his illness or misfortune take in such high dudgeon and be so magnificently aggrieve by that mr
boythorn find himself under the necessity of commit a flagrant trespass to restore his neighbour to himself
similarly mr
boythorn continue to post tremendous placard on the dispute thoroughfare and with his bird upon his head to hold forth vehemently against sir leicester in the sanctuary of his own home similarly also he defy he as of old in the little church by testify a bland unconsciousness of his existence
but it be whisper that when he be most ferocious towards his old foe he be really most considerate and that sir leicester in the dignity of be implacable little suppose how much he be humour

so the quarrel go on to the satisfaction of both
in one of the lodge of the park--that lodge within sight of the house where once upon a time when the water be out down in lincolnshire my lady use to see the keeper s child--the stalwart man the trooper formerly be house
some relic of his old calling hang upon the wall and these it be the choose recreation of a little lame man about the stable-yard to keep gleam bright
a busy little man he always be in the polishing at harness-house door of stirrup-iron bit curb-chain harness boss anything in the way of a stable-yard that will take a polish lead a life of friction
a shaggy little damaged man withal not unlike an old dog of some mongrel breed who have be considerably knock about
he answer to the name of phil
a goodly sight it be to see the grand old housekeeper hard of hear now go to church on the arm of her son and to observe-- which few do for the house be scant of company in these time--the relation of both towards sir leicester and his towards they
they have visitor in the high summer weather when a grey cloak and umbrella unknown to chesney wold at other period be see among the leaf when two young lady be occasionally find gambolling in sequester saw-pit and such nook of the park and when the smoke of two pipe wreathe away into the fragrant evening air from the trooper s door
then be a fife hear troll within the lodge on the inspire topic of the british grenadiers and as the evening close in a gruff inflexible voice be hear to say while two man pace together up and down but i never own to it before the old girl
discipline must be maintain the great part of the house be shut up and it be a show-house no long yet sir leicester hold his shrunken state in the long drawing-room for all that and repose in his old place before my lady s picture
close in by night with broad screen and illumine only in that part the light of the drawing-room seem gradually contract and dwindle until it shall be no more
a little more in truth and it will be all extinguish for sir leicester and the damp door in the mausoleum which shut so tight and look so obdurate will have open and receive he
volumnia grow with the flight of time pinker as to the red in her face and yellower as to the white read to sir leicester in the long evening and be drive to various artifice to conceal her yawn of which the chief and most efficacious be the insertion of the pearl necklace between her rosy lip
long-wind treatise on the buffy and boodle question show how buffy be immaculate and boodle villainous and how the country be lose by be all boodle and no buffy or save by be all buffy and no boodle it must be one of the two and cannot be anything else be the staple of her reading
sir leicester be not particular what it be and do not appear to follow it very closely far than that he always come broad awake the moment volumnia venture to leave off and sonorously repeat her last word beg with some displeasure to know if she find herself fatigue
however volumnia in the course of her bird-like hopping about and peck at paper have alight on a memorandum concern herself in the event of anything happen to her kinsman which be handsome compensation for an extensive course of reading and hold even the dragon boredom at bay
the cousin generally be rather shy of chesney wold in its dullness but take to it a little in the shooting season when gun be hear in the plantation and a few scatter beater and keeper wait at the old place of appointment for low-spirited two and three of cousin
the debilitated cousin more debilitate by the dreariness of the place get into a fearful state of depression groan under penitential sofa-pillow in his gunless hour and protest that such fernal old jail s--nough t sew fler up--frever
the only great occasion for volumnia in this change aspect of the place in lincolnshire be those occasion rare and widely separate when something be to be do for the county or the country in the way of grace a public ball
then indeed do the tuckered sylph come out in fairy form and proceed with joy under cousinly escort to the exhausted old assembly-room fourteen heavy mile off which during three hundred and sixty-four day and night of every ordinary year be a kind of antipodean lumber- room full of old chair and table upside down
then indeed do she captivate all heart by her condescension by her girlish vivacity and by she skip about as in the day when the hideous old general with the mouth too full of tooth have not cut one of they at two guineas each
then do she twirl and twine a pastoral nymph of good family through the maze of the dance
then do the swain appear with tea with lemonade with sandwich with homage
then be she kind and cruel stately and unassuming various beautifully wilful
then be there a singular kind of parallel between she and the little glass chandelier of another age embellish that assembly-room which with their meagre stem their spare little drop their disappointing knob where no drop be their bare little stalk from which knob and drop have both depart and their little feeble prismatic twinkling all seem volumnias
for the rest lincolnshire life to volumnia be a vast blank of overgrown house look out upon tree sighing wring their hand bow their head and cast their tear upon the window- pane in monotonous depression
a labyrinth of grandeur less the property of an old family of human being and their ghostly likeness than of an old family of echoing and thundering which start out of their hundred graf at every sound and go resound through the building
a waste of unused passage and staircase in which to drop a comb upon a bedroom floor at night be to send a stealthy footfall on an errand through the house
a place where few people care to go about alone where a maid scream if an ash drop from the fire take to cry at all time and season become the victim of a low disorder of the spirit and give warning and depart
thus chesney wold
with so much of itself abandon to darkness and vacancy with so little change under the summer shin or the wintry lowering so sombre and motionless always--no flag fly now by day no row of light sparkle by night with no family to come and go no visitor to be the soul of pale cold shape of room no stir of life about it--passion and pride even to the stranger s eye have die away from the place in lincolnshire and yield it to dull repose
chapter lxvii the close of esther s narrative full seven happy year i have be the mistress of bleak house

with the first money we save at home we add to our pretty house by throw out a little growlery expressly for my guardian which we inaugurate with great splendour the next time he come down to see we
i try to write all this lightly because my heart be full in draw to an end but when i write of he my tear will have their way
i never look at he but i hear our poor dear richard call he a good man
to ada and her pretty boy he be the fond father to i he be what he have ever be and what name can i give to that
he be my husband s good and dear friend he be our child s darling he be the object of our deep love and veneration
yet while i feel towards he as if he be a superior being i be so familiar with he and so easy with he that i almost wonder at myself
i have never lose my old name nor have he lose his nor do i ever when he be with we sit in any other place than in my old chair at his side dame trot dame durden little woman--all just the same as ever and i answer yes dear guardian just the same

i think my darling girl be more beautiful than ever

i call he my richard
but he say that he have two mama and i be one
we be not rich in the bank but we have always prosper and we have quite enough
i never walk out with my husband but i hear the people bless he
i never go into a house of any degree but i hear his praise or see they in grateful eye
i never lie down at night but i know that in the course of that day he have alleviate pain and soothe some fellow-creature in the time of need
i know that from the bed of those who be past recovery thank have often often go up in the last hour for his patient ministration
be not this to be rich
the people even praise i as the doctor s wife
the people even like i as i go about and make so much of i that i be quite abash
i owe it all to he my love my pride
they like i for his sake as i do everything i do in life for his sake
a night or two ago after bustle about prepare for my darling and my guardian and little richard who be come to-morrow i be sit out in the porch of all place that dearly memorable porch when allan come home
so he say my precious little woman what be you do here and i say the moon be shin so brightly allan and the night be so delicious that i have be sit here think what have you be think about my dear say allan then
how curious you be say i
i be almost ashamed to tell you but i will
i have be think about my old look--such as they be and what have you be think about them my busy bee say allan
i have be think that i think it be impossible that you could have love i any well even if i have retain they such as they be say allan laugh
such as they be of course my dear dame durden say allan draw my arm through his do you ever look in the glass you know i do you see i do it and donot you know that you be pretty than you ever be i do not know that i be not certain that i know it now
but i know that my dear little pet be very pretty and that my darling be very beautiful and that my husband be very handsome and that my guardian have the bright and most benevolent face that ever be see and that they can very well do without much beauty in i--even supposing--
